source: CIS AZURE KUBERNETES SERVICE (AKS) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF_updated_20250825_190647.json

input_row.title: Ensure latest CNI version is used
input_row.description: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
input_row.rationale: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies. Impact: None.
input_row.audit: Ensure CNI plugin supports network policies. Set Environment Variables to run export RESOURCE_GROUP=Resource Group Name export CLUSTER_NAME=Cluster Name Azure command to check for CNI plugin: az aks show --resource-group ${RESOURCE_GROUP} --name ${CLUSTER_NAME} --query "networkProfile"
input_row.remediation: As with RBAC policies, network policies should adhere to the policy of least privileged access. Start by creating a deny all policy that restricts all inbound and outbound traffic from a namespace or create a global policy using Cilium or Calico. Default Value: This will depend on the CNI plugin in use. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/compute-storage- net/network-plugins/ 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/security/benchmark/azure/security-controls-v2- network-security#ns-1-implement-security-for-internal-traffic 3. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/use-network-policies Additional Information: One example here is Flannel (https://github.com/coreos/flannel) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.
input_row.reference: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/compute-storage- net/network-plugins/ 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/security/benchmark/azure/security-controls-v2- network-security#ns-1-implement-security-for-internal-traffic 3. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/use-network-policies Additional Information: One example here is Flannel (https://github.com/coreos/flannel) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.

gpt_response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The audit approach is determined to be automated for the following reasons:
- The audit field provides a complete Azure CLI command that can be executed to verify the control.
- The control can be checked through Azure Resource Manager APIs, allowing for automated validation of CNI versions.
- The control is specific to network configurations that align with API-accessible settings in Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS).

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_aks_cni_version_verified

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
The automated program would check the following:

- **Azure Resource Manager APIs or Azure CLI commands to use:**
  - Command: `az aks show --resource-group ${RESOURCE_GROUP} --name ${CLUSTER_NAME} --query "networkProfile"`

- **Specific resources to enumerate:**
  - Subscriptions
  - Resource groups
  - AKS clusters

- **Exact configuration properties/settings to validate:**
  - Verify that the CNI plugin in use supports network policies through the `networkProfile`.

- **Clear Pass/Fail criteria with expected values:**
  - **Pass:** The CNI plugin supports network policies (e.g., Calico or Cilium).
  - **Fail:** The CNI plugin does not support network policies (e.g., Flannel).

- **Example API endpoint or CLI command:**
  - Example CLI command for validation:
    ```bash
    az aks show --resource-group myResourceGroup --name myAKSCluster --query "networkProfile"
    ```
  - The output will specify the CNI plugin in use; if it is Flannel without support for network policies, it will indicate a non-compliant state.
